coredata.dc:title: A label-free yellow-emissive carbon dot-based nanosensor for sensitive and selective ratiometric detection of chromium (VI) in environmental water samples
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shengrui
Jin, Lingxia
Liu, Jin
Wang, Qin
Jiao, Long
coredata.dc:description: Measurement of Cr (VI) in environmental water samples is essential for monitoring drinking water quality and the risks of industrial waste. In this study, we report the first use of a new type of label-free carbon dot-based nanosensor for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Cr (VI) in environmental water samples. By using a simple one-pot method based on the solvothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine, bright-yellow-emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) were synthesized, exhibiting two emission bands centered at 430 and 560 nm under a single-wavelength excitation at 345 nm. Fluorescence intensities of the two emission bands of the as-synthesized Y-CDs were found to be simultaneously Cr (VI)-sensitive. The ratio of intensity (I 560 nm/I 430 nm) was linear against Cr (VI) concentrations from 0.01 to 4.5 μM at pH 6.0, and the detection limit (S/N = 3: 1) was 2.3 nM, therefore having a propensity to be a suitable ratiometric probe for sensing Cr (VI). Furthermore, the nanosensor was successfully applied to quantitatively detect Cr (VI) in environmental water samples. Compared with the majority of current ratiometric Cr (VI) sensors, this label-free CD-based ratiometric probe has low toxicity, a simple synthesis route, and excellent photostability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanoprobes
Label-free
Ratiometric sensing
Cr (VI)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cr (VI) in environmental water samples
Determination of Cr (VI) in environmental water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Size and morphology of Y-CDs characterized by TEM image.
The structure characterization of Y-CDs. (A) FT-IR spectrum; (B) XPS survey spectrum.
High-resolution XPS survey scan of (A) C 1s, (B) N 1s, (C) O 1s, and (D) Cl 2p spectra of Y-CDs; Each band was deconvoluted following the literature [35–43].
The absorption (A) and fluorescence emission spectra of Y-CDs under different excitation (B).
Fluorescence of Y-CDs in aqueous solution under different pH levels.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Y-CDs in the presence of different concentrations (0–4.5 μM) of Cr (VI) in 20 mmol/L phosphate buffer at pH 6.0. (B) Linear relationship of the ratiometric fluorescence intensity (I 560 nm/I 430 nm) versus different concentrations of Cr (VI). λex = 345 nm.
Spectroscopic overlap between absorption spectra of Cr (VI), excitation (λem = 560 nm) and emission (λex = 345 nm) bands of Y-CDs in aqueous solution.
Selectivity of Y-CDs to Cr (VI) in the presence of other biologically relevant species (species concentration, 200 μM).
Schematic illustration of Y-CD preparation and their application for ratiometric sensing of Cr (VI).
Determination of Cr (VI) in natural water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A label-free yellow-emissive carbon dot-based nanosensor for sensitive and selective ratiometric detection of chromium (VI) in environmental water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi, 723000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi, 723000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an Shiyou University, Xi'an, 710065, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi'an Shiyou University, Xi'an, 710065, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Measurement of Cr (VI) in environmental water samples is essential for monitoring drinking water quality and the risks of industrial waste. In this study, we report the first use of a new type of label-free carbon dot-based nanosensor for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Cr (VI) in environmental water samples. By using a simple one-pot method based on the solvothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine, bright-yellow-emissive carbon dots (Y-CDs) were synthesized, exhibiting two emission bands centered at 430 and 560 nm under a single-wavelength excitation at 345 nm. Fluorescence intensities of the two emission bands of the as-synthesized Y-CDs were found to be simultaneously Cr (VI)-sensitive. The ratio of intensity (I 560 nm/I 430 nm) was linear against Cr (VI) concentrations from 0.01 to 4.5 μM at pH 6.0, and the detection limit (S/N = 3: 1) was 2.3 nM, therefore having a propensity to be a suitable ratiometric probe for sensing Cr (VI). Furthermore, the nanosensor was successfully applied to quantitatively detect Cr (VI) in environmental water samples. Compared with the majority of current ratiometric Cr (VI) sensors, this label-free CD-based ratiometric probe has low toxicity, a simple synthesis route, and excellent photostability.

ce:section.ce:para: As a metallic element, chromium (Cr) is widely applied in painting, steel manufacturing, welding, leather tanning, and as a catalyst. The widespread use of Cr has resulted in an increase in Cr contamination which has noticeable adverse biological and ecological effects on ecosystems [1,2]. Cr is predominantly found in two states of oxidation: trivalent chromium (Cr (III)) and hexavalent chromium (Cr (VI)). Cr (III) is an essential trace element for human nutrition, whereas Cr (IV) has mutagenetic and carcinogenetic effects on living organisms, having the potential to cause severe human health effects [3,4]. Cr (IV) also has a diffusible cell membrane size [5,6], and concentrations of Cr (VI) in drinking water have been limited to 50 μg/L by the World Health Organization (WHO) [7]. Therefore, in order to monitor drinking water quality and the risks of industrial waste, it is important that a sensitive and fast response analytical method is developed to determination low concentration levels of Cr (VI).

Currently, fluorescence-based methods have been successfully applied to measure Cr (VI) due to their high specificity and sensitivity, nondestructive fast analysis, and high temporal/spatial resolution capability [8–10]. However, the majority of these fluorescent sensors use organic dyes as fluorophore, which may suffer from relatively tedious procedures and rapid photo-bleaching, thereby resulting in difficulties recording long-term imaging. Otherwise, nanomaterial-based fluorescence sensors have become more widely used to measure Cr (VI) due to their excellent physical and chemical properties.

Carbon dots (CDs) have become more important in sensing Cr (VI) due to their low cost, simple manufacturing process, and excellent photo and chemical stability [11–20]. However, nanoprobes are mainly dependent on signal readout and they may fail to accurately quantify concentrations of target analytes due to the existence of various analyte-independent confounding factors, such as instrument parameters and background light scattering from the complex sample matrix [21–24]. These issues can be effectively overcome with ratiometric measurements which offer self-calibration for environmental interference and enable more accurate image contrasting [25–27]. It has been reported that embedding dyes to CDs or covalent cross-linking can be used to construct ratiometric fluorescence signals of CD-based sensors, resulting in time consuming and tedious procedures due to complex purification and separation steps [28–30]. Therefore, the construction of CD-based fluorescence nanoprobes free from labeling and exhibiting ratiometric readout is the threshold of using fluorescence for Cr (VI) sensing in environmental samples.

Toward this end, we developed a label-free yellow-emissive CDs (Y-CDs) as the ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe for measuring Cr (VI) in environmental water samples. The Y-CDs exhibiting dual emissions at 430 and 560 nm were obtained using a one-pot solvothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) with HCl (Scheme 1 ). Intrinsic ratiometric fluorescence emission (I 560 nm/I 430 nm) of the CD-based nanosensor exhibited good linearity with Cr (VI) when concentrations ranged between 0.01 and 4.5 μM. The ratiometric nanoprobe recorded a high level of selectivity for Cr (VI) detection over other cations and anions. By utilizing unique emission properties, Y-CDs were successfully applied as a type of ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe to measure Cr (VI) concentrations in environmental water samples.

Metal salts, including o-PD, were supplied by Aladdin Ltd (Shanghai, China). Other reagents were analytical grade and not purified before use. Distilled water was used throughout the whole experiment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were used to capture images using a GZF 2.0 FEI Electron optics (America). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using an X ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5178 Å). The 2θ scanning range was from 10° to 70° with a scanning speed of 0.1°/s. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was undertaken using an ESCALAB 250Xi multi-technique surface analyzer (Thermo Scientific). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained using a Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with KBr pellets. A Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Japan) was used to record UV–Vis absorption spectra. All fluorescence spectra were analyzed using a RF5301 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

o-PD was carbonized with HCl to synthesize Y-CDs by using a facile solvothermal method. This process was undertaken by: (i) dissolving o-PD (0.1 g) in 10 mL C2H5OH; (ii) 40 μL of concentrated HCl was then gradually added to the o-PD mixture before transferring the solution into a 25 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined stainless steel autoclave; (iii) A brown suspension liquid was obtained by autoclaving the samples for 3 h at 180 °C; (iv) Silica column chromatography was used to purify the mixture using a methylene dichloride solution and methanol as the eluent (CH2Cl2/CH3OH, 20: 1 v/v); (v) The Y-CD sample was finally obtained after the solvent was removed.

QY of the Y-CDs was calculated as:
(1)
∅ x
=
∅
s t
(
D x
D
s t
)
(
A
s t
A x
)
(
n x 2
n
s t
2
)
where, Φ
st is the QY of the standard; D is the area under the emission spectra; A is absorbance at the excitation wavelength; n is the refractive index of the used solvent; x subscript denotes unknown; and st is the standard. The selected standard for our study was Rhodamine B (Φ f = 0.66 in ethanol).

Y-CDs were dissolved in ethanol as the stock solution (1 mg/mL). Fluorescence titration of CDs with Cr (VI) was undertaken as: 2 mL of Y-CD solution (10 μg/mL) was placed into a quartz cuvette. Certain equivalents of Cr (VI) were then added to the CD solution in a stepwise process using a micro-injector. A titration method was used for the fluorescence measurements and the volume added did not exceed 3% of the total. The solution was immediately mixed and incubated (within 60 s) before being excited at 345 nm. During the titration process, all emission values were recorded for analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr (VI) in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water samples from Moat, Tap and Hanjiang water were filtered and the pH was adjusted to 6.0 before analysis. Fluorescence spectra of the pretreated samples spiked with Cr (VI) (0, 2, 4, and 6 μM concentrations) were recorded.

Before CD-based probes with a high level of sensitivity can be constructed, CDs need to be synthesized to have a high QY [31,32]. Thus, we used o-PD dissolved in ethanol as the carbon source to prepare CDs (with the addition of HCl for 3 h). These products were purified using silica column chromatography, resulting in solid Y-CDs having a dark brown color. Obtained Y-CDs had a 21.7 wt % yield, and QY of Y-CDs in water were determined to be 26.7% by using rhodamine B (Φ f = 0.66 in ethanol) as a fluorescence standard, which is suitable to record Cr (VI) in environmental samples.

Firstly, TEM was used to characterize Y-CD morphology (Fig. 1 ). Results indicated that Y-CDs had a spherical shape and a size distribution ranging between 2.3 and 6.8 nm (4.5 nm average size; inset of Fig. 1), showing excellent dispersibility in an aqueous solution. The internal structural characterization of Y-CDs performed by HRTEM image analysis revealed that the Y-CDs exhibited identically well-resolved lattice fringes with interplanar spacings of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene (inset of Fig. 1) [33]. XRD of the CDs has also been added. As shown in Fig. S1, the Y-CDs showed a broader peak centered at approximately 25°, which confirms the graphene structure of Y-CDs [34]. FT-IR and XPS spectra were then used to identify elemental compositions and surface functional groups. FT-IR spectrum results of the Y-CDs (Fig. 2 A) showed that Y-CDs contained the following functional groups or chemical bonds: NH2 (3392 cm−1), aliphatic CH2 (2938 cm−1), OC–O− (1738 cm−1), –CN (1536 cm−1), C–N–C (1348 cm−1), C–O (1030 cm−1) and C–H (738 cm−1). Chemical bonds on Y-CD surfaces were also characterized using XPS (Fig. 2B). Results indicated that carbon (83.59%), oxygen (12.12%), nitrogen (2.98%) and chlorine (1.32%) were the main elements of the desired CDs. Results for the high-resolution spectra (Fig. 3 A–D) indicated that the high-resolution C 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks (285.08, 287.33 and 289.18 eV), corresponding to C–C/CC, C–N/C–O and O–CO bonds, respectively [35,36]. Three peaks were also recorded in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (398.14, 400.39 and 403.38 eV), representing pyridinic N, amino N and graphite N bonds, respectively [37–39]. Oxygenated carbon atoms (CO/C–OH) were attributed to the spectrum peak (531.83 eV) recorded for high-resolution O 1s [40,41]. Additionally, a chlorine signal of benzoquinone and benzene Cl was recorded at 198.88 and 201.48 eV, respectively [42,43], possibly deriving from HCl in the solution. This finding indicates that HCl may react with o-PD via polymerization, oxidation, carbonization and protonation reactions [44]. Results from FT-IR and XPS analyses are consistent, suggesting that –NH2 and –COOH groups are present on Y-CD surfaces, enabling the Y-CDs to have excellent water solubility and potential further application in relation to organisms.

Y-CD optical properties were explored using absorption and fluorescence spectra data (Fig. 4 ). Y-CD solution (Fig. 4A) have an absorption band at 288 nm, representing the π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds [45]. In addition, a broad absorption was observed within the range of 310–500 nm, assigned to the n−π* transition from the surface states of Y-CDs [46]. As shown in Fig. 4B, with the change of excitation wavelength ranging from 325 to 385 nm, Y-CD emission fluorescence intensity at 560 nm continuously increased. However, fluorescence intensity at 430 nm recorded a slight decrease. We therefore selected the excitation wavelength at 345 nm for our analysis, which was facilitated with ratiometric detection for Cr (VI). Moreover, as the emission wavelength of Y-CDs recorded virtually no change when the excitation wavelength ranged from 325 to 405 nm, with an interval of 10 nm, CD emission wavelengths were therefore nearly λex-independent.

The kinetic behavior of Y-CDs toward Cr (VI) was initially examined (Fig. S4). With the introduction of Cr (VI) to the solution of Y-CDs, it can be seen that the fluorescent intensity ratio (I 560 nm/I 430 nm) of the reaction mixture reached a plateau at 2 min and then remained constant. Subsequently, the capability of Y-CDs to detect Cr (VI) was investigated. Results from this analysis (Fig. 6 A) indicated that as Cr (VI) concentration increased, emission intensity at 560 nm gradually decreased, while emissions intensity at 430 nm of Y-CDs almost remained constant. Results for the intensity ratio of the two emission channels (I 560 nm/I 430 nm) (Fig. 6B) exhibited good linearity with Cr (IV) concentrations ranging from 0.01 to 4.5 μM, and the detection limit (S/N = 3: 1) (LOD) was recorded to be 2.3 nM, which is suitable for monitoring Cr (VI) concentrations in drinking water. By enabling environmental errors to be corrected, the dual-emission ratiometric probe improved the accuracy of the current method [48].

To investigate the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime in the absence and presence of Cr (VI) was firstly determined at pH 6.0 by fluorescence lifetime spectroscopy (Fig. S5). In the absence and presence of Cr (VI), the average lifetime values of the Y-CDs and Y-CDs–Cr (VI) were 4.01 ns and 4.85 ns, respectively, indicating that the lifetimes of the Y-CDs are not particularly sensitive to the presence of Cr (VI). Since the resonance energy transfer (RET) mechanism will experimentally manifest a significant decrease of the donor fluorescence lifetime, it was likely that RET did not play an important role in the quenching process in our system [49]. Subsequently, the aggregation status of Y-CDs in the presence of Cr (VI) was checked by TEM (Fig. S6). The size distribution was almost unchanged as compared to that of pure Y-CDs. This result indicated that there was no obvious aggregation and excluded the role of aggregation in fluorescence quenching. For further understanding the quenching mechanism, spectral overlap between the Cr (VI) normalized absorption band and the excitation and emission bands of Y-CDs was performed. Excitation spectrum of Y-CDs had one band at 385 nm (Fig. 7 ), and the Y-CD emission band under excitation at 345 nm was centered at 430 and 560 nm. Meanwhile, Cr (VI) exhibits a broad absorption at 288, 350 and 440 nm. Therefore, Cr (VI) can not only shield excitation light for Y-CDs, it can also absorb emission light from Y-CDs, thus ensuring the highly efficient occurrence of the inner filter effect (IFE), resulting in fluorescence quenching of Y-CDs [50]. In addition, the emission peak of Y-CDs located at 430 nm red-shift to 438 nm after the addition of Cr (VI) (Fig. 6A), which indicated that the high selectivity of CDs for Cr (VI) might also be due to the special coordination interaction between Cr (VI) and the functional groups (such as “N” in imidazole group) existed on CDs surface.

For Cr (VI) sensing in a complex intracellular environment, the selectivity of this nanoprobe was evaluated by incubating Y-CDs with Cr (VI) and other biologically relevant species (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Hg2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, F−, Cl−, Br−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, NO2 −, SCN−, CO3 2−, S2O3 2−, SO3 2−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2− and C2O4 2−) in phosphate buffer. Compared with the addition of Cr (VI) to Y-CD dispersion (Fig. 8 ), no marked changes on fluorescence intensity were observed for Y-CDs toward these interfering substances. This finding shows that there was no noticeable interference to Cr (VI) determination by the other common ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cr (VI) in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of this nanoprobe was evaluated by employing Y-CDs to detect Cr (VI) in real water samples. Analysis of filtered water samples from Moat, Tap and Hanjiang water recorded no Cr (VI) (Table 1 ). By subsequently spiking these samples with different concentrations of Cr (VI) (2, 4 and 6 μM), results recorded using the nanoprobe (Table 1) were consistent with the concentration of Cr (VI) added into the samples. Recovery of Cr (VI) in these samples ranged from 96.50% to 103.25% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) between 1.63% and 3.05%, highlighting a good level of accuracy and precision in the proposed method.

The comparison between this nanosensor and other published CD-based sensors for Cr (VI) detection in excitation and emission wavelength, linear range, LODs, and real sample analysis, was summarized in Table S1 [51–59]. This nanosensor had comparable LOD and can be applied to ratiometric fluorescence detection of Cr (VI) in environmental water samples, which can offer self-calibration for environmental interference and effectively overcome the limitations in the single emission intensity measurements.

In conclusion, we explored label-free Y-CDs as a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Cr (VI). By using o-PD and HCl as the reaction source, Y-CDs with dual emissions (430 and 560 nm) were synthesized using a one-pot solvothermal procedure. Y-CD fluorescence intensity ratio (I 560 nm/I 430 nm) was linear against Cr (VI) concentration, having a strong linear relationship with Cr (VI) values ranging from 0.01 to 4.5 μM. Our proposed method was also successfully applied for detecting Cr (VI) in environmental water samples. In contrast to most of the reported nanomaterials-based ratiometric Cr (VI) sensors which rely on the attachment of additional dyes, the advantages of this CD-based ratiometric probe are low in toxicity, easy to synthesize, and free from labels. We therefore believe that this label-free CD-based nanoprobe can be used as a suitable method for recording Cr (VI) concentrations in environmental water samples.

Shengrui Zhang: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Lingxia Jin: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology. Jin Liu: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology. Qin Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Long Jiao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21807068, 2160313), the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No. 2019JQ060, 2019JQ006), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (No. 2018JM2018), the Scientific Research Plan Projects of Shaanxi Education Department of China (19JK0192) and the Youth Innovative Team Project of Higher Education of Shaanxi Province (No.2019.21).
